coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-based portable fluorescence sensor Utilizing carbon dots for semi-quantification of Microgram-scale microplastics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chunhui
Liu, Fengshan
Yang, Qiusheng
Xu, Yang
coredata.dc:description:
Developing facile analytical methods and portable instruments to quantify existence of microplastics (MPs) in environmental samples is demanding but remains challenging. In this study, a method exploiting carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescence source and XRD quartz plate as substrate was proposed for semi-quantitative MPs based on fluorescence “turn on” strategy. After excitation at 365 nm, non-fluorescence CDs film was brightened with yellow emission in the presence of polyethylene (PE) MPs due to the surface environment change of CDs. For realizing facile detection, smartphone and ImageJ software were used to capture the fluorescence signals of the CDs film. A linear curve was fitted to be
y = 0.31627 x + 0.77396
with a linear range of 2–22 μg. Furthermore, the method was further applied on water samples to demonstrate its sensing capability. Finally, a portable sensor involving this analytical method was proposed. This work not only presents a facile analytical method for quantification of PE MPs, but also provides a tool to apply in the field of MPs management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Microplastics
Polyethylene
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CDs and PE MPs
Luminescence mechanism of CDs combined with PE MPs
Feasibility and condition optimization of sensors
Construction of a portable sensor for PE MPs
Analytical performance of the fluorescence sensor for PE MPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images, (B) HR-TEM image, (C) Size distribution and (D) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (E) XPS survey spectrum, (F) C1s high-resolution spectrum, (G) O1s high-resolution spectrum and (H) N1s high-resolution spectrum of CDs. (I) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, PE MPs and PE MPs stained by CDs (λ ex = 365 nm).
(A) Bright field and (B) under UV 365 nm images of CDs. (C) Mixture of PE MPs and CDs after heating 5 min. (D) Mechanistic investigations and possible CDs structure model.
(A) The fluorescence images of PE MPs standard samples. (B) The linear relationship between fluorescence area and mass of PE MPs.
The fluorescence images of adding standard PE samples (4, 8, 12 μg) in real lake water samples (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of CDs-based sensor for detecting PE MPs.
Determination of PE MPs in lake water samples.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-based portable fluorescence sensor Utilizing carbon dots for semi-quantification of Microgram-scale microplastics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Developing facile analytical methods and portable instruments to quantify existence of microplastics (MPs) in environmental samples is demanding but remains challenging. In this study, a method exploiting carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescence source and XRD quartz plate as substrate was proposed for semi-quantitative MPs based on fluorescence “turn on” strategy. After excitation at 365 nm, non-fluorescence CDs film was brightened with yellow emission in the presence of polyethylene (PE) MPs due to the surface environment change of CDs. For realizing facile detection, smartphone and ImageJ software were used to capture the fluorescence signals of the CDs film. A linear curve was fitted to be
y = 0.31627 x + 0.77396
with a linear range of 2–22 μg. Furthermore, the method was further applied on water samples to demonstrate its sensing capability. Finally, a portable sensor involving this analytical method was proposed. This work not only presents a facile analytical method for quantification of PE MPs, but also provides a tool to apply in the field of MPs management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the emergence of the first plastics called Bakelite in 1907, various plastic products have stepped forth and plastics consuming market has rapidly developed [1]. The global plastics production has reached at 400.3 million tons in 2022 [2]. To reduce the pollution caused by plastics, there are many efforts on recycle them. Zhu et al proposed a kind of chemical-recycled polymer derived from γ-butyrolactone [3]. Zhao et al reported a ruthenium-mediated ring-opening metathesis polymerization of cyclooctene for the manufacture of chemically recyclable polyolefin materials [4]. Although sustainable methods were promoted, chemical recycle, mechanical recycled production and bio-based plastics have reached 0.1 %, 8.9 % and 0.5 %, respectively [2]. However, the accumulation of plastics wastes is still growing in a high speed. The existence of vast plastic wastes especially the tiny size of plastics such as micro- and nano-plastics represents a non-negligible threat to environment and human health [5–8].

Due to the mechanical forces, ultraviolet (UV) radiation and oxidation, plastic products can break into increasingly smaller debris. Microplastics (MPs) are defined as a series of plastic debris with size less than 5 mm [9,10], which can be categorized as primary particles and secondary particles [11,12]. The primary particles are prepared as industrial production such as pellets and microbeads [11], and the secondary particles are derived from fragment and degradation of plastics through natural and UV weathering, mechanical abrasion and biological metabolism [9,12]. MPs have widespread occurred and can be found in sea [10,13], soil [14,15], air [16] and etc. Meanwhile, these MPs, which exist in the environment may cause potential negative impacts attributed with leaching of monomers and additives [17]. In addition, some hitchhikers, such as heavy metals, pathogens, organic pollutants and chemical additives can be adsorbed by MPs and even enter the food chain, which causing great damage to the ecosystem [10]. To assess harmful impacts of MPs and ensure environment safety, the methods of MPs analysis and quantification are required.

Nowadays, promising instrumental methods including FT-IR [18,19], Raman [20,21], scanning electron microscopy [22,23], ICP-MS [24,25] and Pyrolysis GC-MS [26–28] are emerging for MPs analysis. FT-IR and Raman are two favorite methodologies to characterize and identify MPs, profiting from non-destructive mode and detail structural fingerprint. However, the auto-fluorescence and organic matters interference limit the performance of Raman and FT-IR, respectively [29]. Microscopy, such as SEM is employed for observing morphology structure of MPs and tracking analysis with high resolution [30]. With EDS combination, SEM-EDS can obtain additional information of elements in the sample and rapid screening of plastics and non-plastics [31]. Pyrolysis GC-MS is an emerging thermal analysis methodology with superiority of simultaneous analysis for identification and quantification. Nevertheless, expensive price and low throughput have hindered the applications of pyrolysis GC-MS [29,32]. The MPs stained with Nile red can be visualized with high resolution using fluorescence microscopy [33]. However, routine fluorescence microscopy is limited by its low spatial resolution unless the use of confocal fluorescence microscope. Although numerous instrumental methodologies are emerging, large-scale instruments are only suitable for analyzing in lab. Therefore, a portable instrument and method for MPs tracking and quantification is highly desired.

The aims of this study were (1) to propose a facile fluorescence-based method for semi-quantification of polyethylene (PE), avoiding limitation from intrinsic low spatial resolution of fluorescence mode and (2) to design a smartphone-based portable sensor for quantification of MPs without large-scale instruments auxiliary. We prepared a kind of CDs derived from nitrated pyrene via solvothermal process and column chromatography separation. Quartz plate commonly-used in XRD characterization was employed as substrate to form CDs film. Aqueous solution contained microgram amount of PE MPs was dripped on the CDs film to realize the coordination between CDs and PE, along with the appearance of yellow fluorescence emission. A linear curve was fitted to be y = 0.31627x + 0.77396 with a mass linear range of 2–22 μg. Furthermore, the method was further applied on water samples to demonstrate its sensing capability. Finally, a smartphone sensor based on this analytical method was proposed. This work not only presents a facile analytical method for quantification of PE MPs, but also provides a tool to apply in the field of MPs management.

Pyrene was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Nitric acid (HNO3, AR) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Boric acid was purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyethylene microplastics (PE MPs) was obtained from Shanghai Runhong International Trade Co., Ltd. Aqueous solution of Fe3+ was prepared from chloride salts, aqueous solution of Cu2+ was prepared from sulfate salts, they both bought from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Quartz flakes with 20 mm round groove were purchased from Zhejiang Heben Pesticide & Chemicals Co., Ltd. The solvents such as N,N-dimethylformamide, petroleum ether, carbon tetrachloride, tetrahydrofuran, methyl alcohol and ethyl acetate were purchased from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. H2O2 (30 wt%) was brought from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The real lake water samples were obtained from Dicui Lake, which was located in Hebei University of Technology (Tianjin, China). The ultrapure water was obtained from an ultrapure water system (ZPE-20) of Tengzhou Zhuopu Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd. None of the above materials has been further purified.

The morphology of CDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 keV. The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using the instruments from Thermo Scientific (ESCALAB 250Xi) and Bruker (TENSOR 27), respectively. Ultraviolet and visible spectrum (UV, Shimadzu UV-26001) was used to observe the absorption spectrum of CDs. The nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer was from bruker biospin AG (NMR, AVANCE 400) to obtain detail information of nitropyrene. The particle size of polyethylene MPs was obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova Nano SEM450). A fluorescence spectrometer was from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd (FL, FLS 1000) to obtain the fluorescence spectra of CDs and MPs.

Pyrene-based CDs were prepared via two-steps involving nitropyrene preparation and solvothermal reaction. Firstly, nitropyrene was synthesized according to the reported methods [34]. 1 g of pyrene was added into nitric acid solution (80 mL), followed with refluxing at 80 °C for 12 h. As-prepared yellow powder was obtained by deionized water washing numerous times. The product was purified by column chromatography with ethyl acetate and petroleum ether (EA:PE (v/v) = 1:1) as eluents to obtain nitropyrene, whose structure was verified using 1H NMR characterization (Fig. S1). According to previous work, the CDs was synthesized with some modification [35]. The nitropyrene (20 mg) and boric acid (50 mg) were dissolved in DMF/water solution. Polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor was used for carbonization under 200 °C for 6 h, and black solid was obtained after drying. Finally, the purification was performed by column chromatography using eluent (EA:PE (v/v) = 1:5). The goal was to collect a component that could stain PE microplastics quickly, which was eventually selected to collect a low polarity component with yellow emission upon UV light stimulation (Fig. S2). The CDs were collected using rotary evaporation to remove excess solvent.

In this study, we designed quartz sheet-based detection assay using pyrene-based CDs for PE MPs quantification due to rapid MPs staining efficiency of the CDs. The CDs was dissolved in DMF to form a working solution with concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. The groove on quartz sheet was covered using 80 μL of the CDs working solution followed by oven drying for vaporing solvent. The MPs suspension (200 μL) with different concentrations (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, and 0.11 mg/mL) were doped onto the modified quartz groove. After drying under 80 °C, a strong yellow fluorescence can be observed from the groove region under 365 nm UV light. The readout system used a smartphone to take pictures under 365 nm excitation conditions. The power of UV 365 nm light was 10 W. The photographic condition was set with ISO = 1250, EV = 0, F = 1.9, and the focal length was 27 mm. Furthermore, a portable homemade camera obscura framework was constructed for photographing to isolate light interference from other items, which ensured that the quartz sheet kept in the same position each time.

Fluorescence data was analyzed by ImageJ software [36]. First, the fluorescence images were treated in ImageJ to separate the channels and selected the green channel. Then, locking sleeve function was used to select the limited luminous part. Finally, the luminescent area was measured using the analytical function, the quality of the PE MPs can be determined by the size of the luminous area, the detection process was shown in Scheme 1 . The same algorithm was used to ensure the reliability of the analysis results. The investigation of the PE MPs solution in presence of H2O2 and ions (Fe3+, Cu2+) were preformed to value the oxidation resistance and ion stability of the sensor, respectively. Ethanol and ethyl acetate were used to investigate the stability of the detector against organic solvents. Three different batches of CDs were used to verify the effectiveness of the luminescence and the reliability of the fluorescence area. The real water samples were obtained from Dicui lake water in the campus of Hebei University of Technology (Tianjin, China). All the experiments mentioned above were carried out three times.

Fluorescence staining for MPs research has been developed as Nile red was found with excellent staining efficiency [33]. Although fluorescence staining faces with low identification accuracy compared with FT-IR and Raman spectroscopies, it exhibits emerging potentials in tracing and quantification of MPs [37–39]. In this study, a CDs-based fluorescence sensor was constructed using a quartz sheet with groove. As low spatial resolution limits accuracy of fluorescence sensing [33], we tried to reform the sensing mode through dispense treatment similar to test paper. The sensing thickness is decreased as far as possible, reducing spatial interference efficiently to realize minimum detectable amount and high throughput.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and PE MPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were collected through solvothermal reaction and column chromatography separation. TEM images were obtained to characterize the morphology of these CDs, exhibiting mono-disperse and a size range of 1–7 nm (Fig. 1A and S3). The lattice spacing of CDs was about 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) inter-planar of graphite [40], indicating that the CDs had a certain graphite-like crystal structure (Fig. 1 B), and the average particle size was 3.95 nm (Fig. 1C). The CDs were also characterized by FT-IR to elucidate the surface functional groups of CDs. The peaks at 3150–3500 cm−1, 960–1150 cm−1, 1732 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 were belonged to the stretching vibration of –OH/–NH, C–O, C=O and N–O bands [40–42]. Due to the addition of boric acid in the synthesis process, the characteristic peaks of boron elements can be observed from the spectrum. The CDs exhibited absorption bands at 1187 and 1090 cm−1, which can be attributed to B–O–H bending vibration and C–B stretching mode, respectively [43]. This indicated that boron was successfully doped and involved in bonding (Fig. 1D). For UV absorption spectrum, the strongest absorption peak near 240 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond, arising from CDs “core state” of sp2-networked carbogenic domain [42]. The absorption peak at 384 nm can be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of the C=O and C–N bonds (Fig. S4).

At the same time, the XPS spectra provided information about the composition of atoms within the CDs, which mainly composed of C (74.89 atom%), O (20.69 atom%), N (2.92 atom%) and B (1.55 atom%) (Fig. 1E). The Si signal came from the silica powder used in the separation of CDs by column chromatography. The C1s spectrum was fitted by three peaks at 284.5, 285.0 and 288.8 eV, which belonged to C–C/C=C, C–O and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1F) [44]. The O1s spectrum was fitted by two peaks at 531.9 and 533.5 eV, corresponded to C=O and C–O, respectively (Fig. 1G) [45]. The N1s spectrum displayed three peaks at 399.2, 404.6 and 406.0 eV, which can attribute to pyridinic–N, graphitic–N and N–O bonds (Fig. 1H), respectively [35]. Overall, the functional groups on the surface of the CDs further confirmed the previous FT-IR data.

For MPs used in the experiment, the SEM image indicated it has a size range between 1–28 μm (Fig. S5A), and the average particle size was 6.8 μm (Fig. S5B). The PE MPs was well dispersed in water through ultrasound treatment for following experiments (Fig. S5C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence mechanism of CDs combined with PE MPs
ce:section.ce:para: Through the solid fluorescence image, we found these initial CDs powder exhibited no apparent fluorescence due to the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons nitropyrene composed with a planar π-conjugated structure which formed H-aggregation [46]. The π-π stacking interaction between the layers resulted in energy transfer, without the need for radiative transitions to consume energy, the aggregation caused quenching fluorescence (Fig. 2 A and B). When PE MPs were introduced into the CDs powder, a new yellow fluorescence that emission peak centered at 567 nm appeared (Fig. 1I and 2C) and the fluorescence quantum yield has significantly increased (Fig. S6), this fluorescence phenomenon might be attributed to the surface micro-environment changing of CDs, similar to solvatochromism [47–49]. Fluorescence solvatochromism was found in dispersed CDs and induced by dipole interactions between the CDs surface states and solvent, incorporate hydrogen bonding interaction, indicated that the fluorescence solvatochromism of CDs could be controlled through solvent polarity. Similar to the organic fluorescence dye Nile Red, the change of environmental polarity influenced their fluorescence emission, thus separate different kinds of plastics according to the hydrophobicity could be achieved. In this work, CDs possessed solvochromism, the existence of solute–solvent interactions resulted in different fluorescence emission (Fig. S7) and could be regulated by the synergistic effect of interlayer π-π stacking and hydrogen bond interaction. In nonpolar solvent, the fluorescence came from the monomer states, with the increase of solvents polarity, hydrogen bond interaction decreased and π-π stacking enhanced, resulting the fluorescence quenching [46]. The results indicated that the solvent polarity was crucial adjective to fluorescence behavior because polarity could change the surface micro-environment of CDs. As CDs interacted with PE MPs, the molecules thermal movement brought CDs into the polyethylene molecules and CDs gradually seeping into the polymer void. In order to investigate the possible mechanism of fluorescence production, the theoretical calculation was carried out. Nitropyrene provided rich π-conjugated systems for CDs and made them an electron-rich system that had the potential to be an electron donor and polymer usually considered to be an electron accepter [50], therefore, intermolecular charge transfer (CT) and recombination might be the key to explain fluorescence. To clarify the CT process between the CDs and polyethylene polymer, proceeded a theoretical calculation based on density functional theory (DFT) and chose basis set at B3LYP, 6-31G(d) level through Gaussian 09. According to the characterization of CDs structure and functional group content, the model of CDs molecule was constructed, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were acquired by calculation (Fig. S8). Because of the great difficulty in calculating the polymer molecular orbital energy level, a monomer of PE MPs (ethylene molecule) was chosen for analysis. The results of molecular orbital energy level indicated that with the increase of the monomer number of ethylene, the HOMO of ethylene combinations gradually increased and the LUMO of them gradually decreased, the energy gap of ethylene combinations gradually decreased (Fig. S9). The energy levels were so close between the HOMOs of the electron donor and LUMOs of the electron accepter that the CT process happened (Fig. 2D). Thus, charge transfer and recombination occurred between the polymer and CDs, the existence of abundant structural and surface defects of CDs could efficiently capture the photoexcited carriers and introduce a fast non-radiative relaxation, this process introduced new energy levels leading to electron transition back to the ground-state and fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, the existence of polymer decreased the π-π stacking interaction, the reduction of aggregation led to the enhancement of fluorescence. Similar to solvent effect, the polyethylene chain spread out CDs, resulted a yellow fluorescence that the emission peak centered at 567 nm appeared. The different plastics provided different polarity environment, leading to various emission color, this phenomenon had been confirmed by previous work [35,45]. Due to the similar polarity, CDs dissolved in CCl4 exhibited fluorescence emission peak at 562 nm, which had the most similar behavior to PE MPs (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and condition optimization of sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence results demonstrated that the CDs have great potential as a “turn-on” fluorescent probe. Compared with the CDs without doping B, CDs in this work exhibited more superior staining performance for PE MPs under the same time period (Fig. S12). In addition, CDs were insoluble in water and they can still maintain staining effect in aqueous solution (Fig. S13). Therefore, we made an assumption that these CDs can be dropped onto the groove followed by staining treatment to construct sensing unit. Since thin connect thickness between CDs and PE MPs, low detectable amount and spatial interference was expected to be realized using this fluorescence sensor.

The quantitative analysis of MPs is a hot research topic in analytical chemistry due to the hazard of MPs. Recently, Pyrolysis GC/MS has been an effective route for quantitation of MPs with sample destructive [32]. Therefore, finding other kinds of methods is equally vital for MPs analysis. Fluorescence as a series of analytical method has been widely used in sensing with high sensitivity. However, the fluorescence sensing still faces with low spatial resolution. Nowadays, some efforts have been taken to realize MPs analysis, decreasing spatial resolution interference [33,45]. As limitation by vacuum, the suction filtration is not suitable for portable analysis. To solve this problem, an unsophisticated fluorescence sensor was proposed for PE MPs detection using quartz plate with groove. This fluorescence sensor is small in size and easy to carry for field analysis. The CDs solution was dropped on the plate to form sensing unit after staining treatment. When an aqueous solution contained PE MPs was dropped on this sensing unit, yellow fluorescence emission appeared within 5 min.

To determine the optimal CDs concentration, the effect of different concentrations of CDs on the quartz sheet was observed under UV light. With the decrease of the concentration of CDs, the background fluorescence gradually weakened, to reduce the impact of background interference on subsequent experiments, 0.1 mg/mL of CDs were finally selected (Fig. S14A). In addition, the stability of the film was essential that the quartz sheet coated with CDs was soaked in water for 6 h. There has no significant change, proving that the CDs have a good stability to water (Fig. S14B). In order to test the fluorescence stability, before and after 1 h irradiation under UV lamp, the luminous area of PE MPs was tested to be 27.29 and 26.68 %, respectively. Proved that this method had excellent anti-photobleaching capability and good fluorescence response (Fig. S15). As shown in Fig. S16A, the obtained signal was increased in the temperature range from 60-80 °C because of the heat accelerated the molecular motion resulting in a more pronounced fluorescence effect, and reached plateau beyond 80 °C. For response time, when the solvent water evaporated, yellow fluorescence can be immediately observed. However, the prolongation of heating time led to the decrease of fluorescence area, this might due to the loss of MPs in a heated air stream because of their extremely light mass, so selected immediately detection after solvent evaporation, this process took 5 min (Fig. S16B). Therefore, 80 °C of reaction temperature and 5 min were chosen as optimal conditions for the analysis process. The acid-base resistance of the sensor was tested. When the pH of PE MPs aqueous solution was between 1–13, the sensor could maintain a relatively stable response signal, indicating that it had a good acid-base resistance (Fig. S16C). Therefore, choose a neutral water sample for the analysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a portable sensor for PE MPs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above optimized working conditions, we constructed a portable instrument that the size was 8.1 × 8 × 5.5 cm as a dark box (Fig. S17). Two holes were used for UV lamp excitation and signal acquisition of smartphone, respectively. Although the instrument seems crude, the superiors of cheap price (about $12), easy-to-carry, and low minimum detectable amount demonstrate this portable platform is an effective tool and has a great potential for real-time detection and tracing of PE MPs.

The sensing performance of this sensor was evaluated using different amounts of PE MPs. The fluorescence images were captured using smartphone, and data analysis was performed by ImageJ software [36]. The obtained fluorescence area data showed a good linear relationship with the amount of PE MPs added (from 2 to 22 μg) and the linear equation was
y = 0.31627 x + 0.77396
(Fig. 3 and Table S1). Notably, the low detection mass of PE MPs was 2 μg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorescence sensor for PE MPs
ce:section.ce:para: PE MPs solution in presence of H2O2 (0.1 wt%) was tested, obtained images and analysis fluorescence area, the results were consistent with the liner equation, which proved that the good oxidation resistance of the sensor (Fig. S18 and Table S2). The influence of metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+) on the process of CDs staining MPs were also investigated, no significant change in staining speed, color and area was observed, proved a good resistance to ion interference of the sensor (Fig. S19 and Table S3). Tested the solvent stability of fluorescence, 50 μL solvent were added and captured images before and after solvent evaporation. Fluorescence area had varying degrees of decline, this might due to the redistribute of solvent in groove, resulted in unevenly distributed of CDs and decrease of fluorescence ability (Fig. S20 and Table S4). The fluorescence effect remained stable through multiple experiments, used different batches of CDs to detect a same concentration of PE MPs and the result conformed to the linear equation (Fig. S21 and Table S5).

In this work, we constructed a smartphone-based analysis sensor for PE MPs detection exploiting CDs as fluorescence source and quartz plate as substrate. A fluorescence “Turn-on” phenomenon could be observed, ascribed to surface environment change of CDs. Smartphone and ImageJ software were used to capture fluorescence signal and analysis the fluorescence area, respectively. No large-scale instruments were required. We ascertained the linear relationship between fluorescence area and PE MPs amount. Notably, the low minimum detectable amount was 2 μg, attributed to excellent staining capability and the thin connect thickness between CDs and MPs. Furthermore, a portable instrument was proposed for further real-time analysis. The outcomes of this study provide a new route to quantitative analysis of MPs, including portable platform design, and inspire further exploration of early warning platform construction for MPs pollution in the environment.

Chunhui Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Fengshan Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Qiusheng Yang: Writing – review & editing. Yang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (No. B2021202056) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.114574.

ce:section.ce:para:
